title: Warsh Signals Hawkish Fed, Iran Oil Flows Resume, ECB Splits on Next Move
subhead: Fed Chair Warsh keeps a July rate hike on the table at Sintra, Iran's sanctioned oil re-enters global markets under a 60-day waiver, and ECB policymakers diverge on whether to hike again as energy costs ease but inflation sticks.

sections.heading: Warsh Fed Keeps July Rate Hike in Play as Inflation Stays 'Too High'
sections.body:
Fed Chair Kevin Warsh used his first major international appearance at the ECB Forum in Sintra, Portugal, on July 1 to deliver an unambiguous message: inflation remains 'too high' and the Fed will deliver price stability. Warsh declined to give specific forward guidance on the July 28-29 FOMC meeting, but markets heard hawkishness. The probability of a rate hike at the next meeting climbed to roughly 1 in 3, with September hike odds spiking to 67%, according to CME FedWatch data cited by CNBC. The current federal funds target range sits at 3.50%-3.75%, with the Fed's preferred core PCE gauge at 3.4% in May and headline inflation at 4.1% — both far above the 2% target.

Warsh's stance dispelled the widespread narrative that he was installed by Trump to ease monetary policy. Instead, he invoked 'price stability' a dozen times in a single press conference and said the Fed would use new data technologies to improve real-time economic monitoring. JPMorgan's Bob Michael flagged July 29 as a potential hike date, while Goldman Sachs' Lindsay Rosner said there is a 'decent chance' of a July increase. However, Brown Brothers Harriman's Scott Clemons pushed back, arguing the Fed may hold off given the election-year environment and already-shifting inflation dynamics.

Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey, also at Sintra, flagged rising leverage in government bond markets, equity markets (especially hedge fund and ETF leverage), and private credit as tail risks that could trigger broader financial instability.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's hawkish debut signals the Fed under his leadership will prioritize inflation fighting over the rate cuts Trump has publicly demanded — a notable assertion of central bank independence. A July hike would be the first move since Warsh took the chair and would reshape rate expectations for the rest of 2026.

sections.heading: Iran Oil Sanctions Lifted Under 60-Day Waiver, Unlocking Billions for Tehran
sections.body:
The US formally waived oil sanctions on Iran for a 60-day period under the interim US-Iran peace deal, allowing Tehran to resume crude exports, petrochemical sales, and transactions in US dollars. Vessels, shipping, and insurance companies involved with Iranian oil are also no longer under US sanctions for the waiver period, which expires August 21 and can be renewed by mutual consent.

According to NPR, citing Energy Aspects strategist Mohammad Darwazah, Iran could generate $8-10 billion in oil revenue during the waiver window. Darwazah described the breadth of the waivers as surprising: 'There is no trust between both sides. So for the Iranians to get this as the first step is quite significant.' The waivers extend beyond crude to include petrochemicals and other petroleum products.

Bob McNally, president of Rapidan Energy Group, characterized the deal bluntly: 'This is a ransom payment for oil. Iran took Hormuz hostage, and we had to pay Iran to release the hostage.' S&P Global's Jim Burkhard noted that Iran can now sell openly rather than relying on shadow fleets, likely at better prices than the discounted rates it was forced to accept from Chinese refiners under sanctions. Iran has the capacity to move roughly 2 million barrels per day — about 2% of the global market.

Trump defended the deal at the G7, saying: 'I didn't want to see economic catastrophe. If you kept this going, that could have happened.' However, Iran hawks in Congress have raised concerns that the regime will use oil revenue to rebuild its military and proxy networks. Reuters reported that the IRGC's business empire stands to win big from the sanctions relief.
sections.whyItMatters: The waiver is the most significant US sanctions relief on Iranian oil in nearly a decade, and it transforms the near-term supply picture for global crude. But the 60-day sunset and the IRGC's control over much of Iran's commercial infrastructure mean this is as much a geopolitical gambit as an economic policy — Tehran has every incentive to comply just long enough to lock in the revenue.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Return to Pre-War Levels, but Supply Chain Recovery Lags
sections.body:
Brent crude has slipped back to the $71-73 per barrel range, roughly where it traded before the US-Iran war began in late February. West Texas Intermediate is hovering around $68-70, erasing most of the war premium that pushed prices above $90 at peak conflict. UBS cut its 2026 and 2027 oil price forecasts on July 2, citing the recovery of Hormuz transit volumes to approximately 50% of pre-conflict levels and the return of Iranian crude exports.

However, a Reuters Breakingviews analysis warns that tanker transit data tells a more cautious story than spot prices. While Brent at $72 suggests the crisis is over, freight rates for Hormuz shipping remain elevated, and the 'sugar rush' from clearing stuck cargoes masks an underlying supply deficit. Demining operations in the Strait of Hormuz could take 40-50 days before insurers and shipping companies feel confident enough to resume normal traffic, according to one analysis cited in the headlines data.

The Economist reported that even with the US-Iran peace accord, oil prices are likely to stay elevated for months as physical supply chains and shipping routes take time to normalize. S&P Global's Burkhard noted that some buyers remain wary given that the US and Iran continue to trade blows, putting the deal in jeopardy.
sections.whyItMatters: The oil price recovery looks clean on the surface but the physical market tells a different story. Freight rates, demining timelines, and buyer wariness mean the real supply recovery will lag the spot price signal by weeks or months — a gap that could widen again if the Iran deal falters.

sections.heading: ECB Splits on Next Move as Energy Prices Fall but Inflation Persists
sections.body:
The ECB is publicly divided on whether to follow up its 25 basis point hike on June 11 — its first rate change since 2023, bringing the deposit facility rate to 2.25% — with another increase as soon as July. ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the central bank will remain 'proactive' in fighting high inflation even after the Iran deal brought down energy prices. Belgian central bank governor Pierre Wunsch, who on June 19 said a July hike was still in play, shifted his stance by June 30, telling Bloomberg the case for another hike 'is not as strong now'.

ECB Governing Council member Gabriel Makhlouf warned that an interim deal to end the Middle East conflict will not necessarily bring an immediate end to the energy shock, noting that damage to energy infrastructure could cause lingering price pressures. Meanwhile, a Bloomberg analysis on July 2 described the ECB as 'split on next rate move as inflation sinks', with some policymakers pointing to easing commodity prices and others pointing to still-sticky core inflation.

The ECB's dilemma is acute: eurozone growth is faltering, but the energy shock has fed through into broader inflation that the June hike was designed to address. The Iran deal's deflationary effect on oil may take pressure off, but Makhlouf's warning about damaged energy infrastructure means the inflation impulse may not fade as quickly as markets hope.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB's split reflects a genuine dilemma — falling energy prices argue for pause, but persistent core inflation argues for another hike. Which side prevails at the July 24 meeting will signal whether the ECB prioritizes recession risk or price stability.

sections.heading: G7 Ramps Up Pressure on Russia: New Sanctions on Shadow Fleet, Energy Revenue
sections.body:
The UK unveiled a package of 70 new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, military procurement supply chains, and illicit finance networks at the G7 summit. British troops seized a Russian-flagged oil tanker in the English Channel — the first such seizure by UK forces — underscoring the operational intensity of the new enforcement. The sanctions also hit Yandex Bank and networks linked to Russian military intelligence.

Canada announced its own package targeting 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector, following PM Mark Carney's meeting with Zelenskyy at the G7. Starmer also pledged new nuclear energy support for Ukraine alongside the sanctions.

Trump signaled he may swiftly reimpose sanctions on Russian oil, telling G7 leaders that Russia should 'make a deal' on Ukraine. The G7 communique referenced persistent global imbalances — widely understood to refer to China — and agreed to intensify pressure on Russia to end the war.

Meanwhile, Russia is feeling the economic strain: the country has slashed domestic fuel quality standards as Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries squeeze domestic supply, a sign that Western sanctions and Ukrainian military pressure are compounding on the Russian energy sector.
sections.whyItMatters: The coordinated sanctions escalation on Russia's shadow fleet is the most significant enforcement action in months, and the UK's seizure of a vessel marks a new willingness to use kinetic enforcement rather than just legal tools. Trump's signal on Russian oil sanctions, if followed through, would tighten the screws further.

sections.heading: US Delays DeepSeek Blacklist Amid China AI Tensions; G7 Squares Off on Trade Imbalances
sections.body:
The Trump administration has delayed adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and over 100 Chinese companies flagged as national security risks to the Commerce Department's Entity List, despite an interagency committee approving the designations eight months ago. The delay, reported by Yahoo News, reflects Washington's effort to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing at a moment when trade negotiations are ongoing. DeepSeek and CXMT were tagged for supporting Chinese military and intelligence operations.

At the G7 summit, Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi urged allies to develop a united front against Beijing's use of export controls and trade leverage, proposing a joint critical minerals stockpiling initiative. She also pushed for coordinated G7 action on China's trade tactics. AP reported that China notched a record $1.2 trillion global trade surplus last year despite eight years of US tariffs, with Chinese exports redirecting toward Europe and Asia — what Macron called 'literally killing a large part of European industry.'

The G7 communique did not name China but referenced 'persistent and widening global imbalances.' Carney and Trump also discussed China EV tariffs at the summit. Meanwhile, the US also forced Anthropic to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models citing security concerns, drawing criticism that AI regulation is becoming ad hoc and weaponized.
sections.whyItMatters: The DeepSeek delay illustrates the tension between national security imperatives and diplomatic positioning — Washington has the blacklist ready but won't publish it while it needs Chinese cooperation on trade. The G7's inability to name China directly in its communique tells you everything about where consensus ends and disagreement begins.

sections.heading: Central Banks Accelerate Gold Buying as Dollar Reserve Confidence Wanes
sections.body:
The World Gold Council's 2026 Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey, released June 16, found that 89% of central banks expect global gold reserves to rise over the next 12 months, while a record 45% of 76 central banks surveyed plan to increase their own gold holdings — more than double the 20% share recorded in 2020. Most strikingly, more central banks plan to reduce dollar holdings than increase them over the next decade for the first time in the survey's history.

The trend is driven by geopolitical instability, inflation concerns, and what the Financial Express described as the strategic importance of gold as a hedge against sanctions risk and reserve currency politicization. Central bank gold holdings are already at a 50-year high, and Goldman Sachs projects that stronger-than-expected official sector buying will continue, helping gold prices recover by year-end.

The dedollarization signal is significant: it's no longer just BRICS countries talking about diversifying away from the dollar. The survey captures a broad shift in reserve management philosophy among Western-aligned central banks as well, driven in part by the weaponization of the dollar-based financial system through sanctions on Russia and Iran.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most concrete data point yet that dedollarization is moving from rhetoric to reserve allocation decisions. When 45% of central banks are actively adding gold and more plan to cut dollars than add them, the dollar's exorbitant privilege is being tested in real time.

bottomLine: The post-Iran-war financial landscape is taking shape, and it is more fragmented than before. Warsh's Fed is signaling it may hike rates as early as July despite Trump's demands for cuts, asserting independence at a moment when the White House needs monetary easing most. Iran's oil is flowing again under a 60-day waiver that generates $8-10 billion for Tehran — a price the US paid to reopen Hormuz, but one that hands the IRGC a financial lifeline. The ECB is split on whether to hike again, caught between falling energy prices and sticky core inflation. Oil has returned to pre-war levels on the surface, but the physical market — freight rates, demining, buyer caution — tells a slower recovery story. Meanwhile, the G7 escalated sanctions enforcement on Russia's shadow fleet, the US delayed blacklisting Chinese AI firms to preserve diplomatic space, and central banks worldwide are voting with their reserves: more gold, fewer dollars. The common thread across all of these developments is that the geopolitical shocks of the past four months have permanently altered the calculus of monetary policy, sanctions architecture, and reserve management — and the adjustments are just beginning.
sources: CNBC, NPR, Reuters, Bloomberg, AP News, Yahoo News, S&P Global (via NPR), Rapidan Energy Group (via NPR), Energy Aspects (via NPR), UBS, World Gold Council, Financial Express, South Florida Reporter, CryptoBriefing, The Economist, Anadolu Agency, The Guardian, MSN, Mirror, DPA On The Net
